message.content:
Read /home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_BRIEF_V4.md IN FULL. It is the complete measured record. Then design ONE SINGLE COMPLETE PLAN to **BEAT 0.5578**.

THE OPERATOR'S DIRECTION, VERBATIM: "Why stick with bodhi class? Why not the top one? ... If we have any old rules
that hurt us from score, just drop it. If we don't have enough corpus to make us SOTA, then kick on the agentic
outline search, or we do better at the early query generation + search + fetch + select + weight + dedup +
consolidate, make them much much much better and richer so we are SOTA. Don't stuck with anything. I need one
single solid plan, unleash the full power at the beginning, then keep the hamster wheel running till we match SOTA
and beat SOTA."

**EVERY PREVIOUS PLAN — INCLUDING YOURS — QUIETLY LOWERED THE BAR TO MATCH WHAT HAD ALREADY BEEN BUILT.** Your v2
plan capped at 0.535-0.552 and declared 0.5578 "aggressive". Fable's capped at 0.48. Opus rationalised a 45-paper
corpus as "bodhi-class" because bodhi wins with 33. **THAT IS THE FAILURE MODE. DESIGN FOR #1.**

THE TWO CORRECTIONS THAT UNLOCK THIS:
1. **THE FAITHFULNESS LINE WAS DRAWN WRONG** (brief §3). bodhi's winning sentence CITES TWO SOURCES — an empirical
   null from one paper and a diffusion-lag MECHANISM stated by another. It is not fabricating; it is citing a theory
   paper for the mechanism and an empirical paper for the finding. **Our gate kills it because it checks the UNION
   of spans for literal presence.** The corrected line: a mechanism may be asserted if ANY cited source states it.
   Fabricating facts/numbers/attributions stays forbidden forever.
2. **OUR RETRIEVAL IS AIMED WRONG, NOT SHALLOW.** 997 rows / 919 URLs — but only 17 on-topic journal articles; the
   enriched corpus contains ResNet, the BMJ PRISMA checklist, and a 1974 Cognitive Psychology paper on reading.
   **We have an agentic retrieval loop (PG_AGENTIC_*, outline_agent) that was never pointed at this.** And keyword
   search PROVABLY cannot find this literature (Autor-Levy-Murnane, the field's most important paper, contains
   neither "AI" nor "labor market" in its title). **Citation-graph expansion does.**

Design the whole thing: retrieval at the root, the evidence layer, the synthesis lane, the composer, the rules to
DROP, and the wheel that runs until we beat 0.5578. Ground everything in the rubric text or a measured artifact.
Read the winners' artifacts at /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board and our code at /home/polaris/wt/flywheel. Be honest about the ceiling — but do not lower the bar.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# BRIEF V4 — THE MISSION IS TO **BEAT** 0.5578. ONE SINGLE PLAN. UNLEASH EVERYTHING.
2	
3	## 0. THE OPERATOR'S DIRECTION (this is the frame; do not narrow it)
4	> "Why stick with bodhi class? Why not the top one? ... If we have any old rules that hurt us from score, just
5	> drop it. If we don't have enough corpus to make us SOTA, then kick on the agentic outline search, or we do
6	> better at the early query generation + search + fetch + select + weight + dedup + consolidate, make them
7	> much much much better and richer so we are SOTA. Don't stuck with anything. I need one single solid plan,
8	> unleash the full power at the beginning, then keep the hamster wheel running till we match SOTA and beat SOTA."
9	
10	**TARGET: > 0.5578 (cellcog-max, #1). NOT parity. NOT bodhi. THE TOP.**
11	Every previous plan (mine, Fable's, Sol's) quietly lowered the bar to match what had already been built.
12	**That is the failure mode to avoid. Design for #1.**
13	
14	## 1. THE BOARD (task 72, our evaluator = GPT-5.5, from the benchmark's own per-task raw_results)
15	| system | OVERALL | compreh | INSIGHT | instr-follow | readability |
16	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
17	| **cellcog-max** | **0.5578** (corpus-wide #1; no per-task breakdown published — its task-72 score may be HIGHER than bodhi's) | 0.5634 | 0.5708 | 0.5530 | 0.5194 |
18	| bodhi (best *known* task-72 score) | 0.5441 | 0.5441 | 0.5457 | 0.5559 | 0.5199 |
19	| lunon | 0.5406 | | 0.5594 | | |
20	| WhaleCloud | 0.5396 | | | | |
21	| gemini-2.5-pro-DR = **THE REFERENCE** (parity=0.50) | 0.5102 | 0.5111 | 0.5035 | 0.5187 | 0.5100 |
22	| openai-DR | 0.4743 | | | | |
23	| **POLARIS** | **0.4382** | 0.4549 | 0.4238 | 0.4409 | **0.3774** (worst on the entire board) |
24	Weights: **INSIGHT 0.32 | COMPREHENSIVENESS 0.29 | INSTRUCTION-FOLLOWING 0.25 | READABILITY 0.14.**
25	Measurement: judge SD **0.0074**; k=5 paired => **smallest resolvable effect +0.0094**. NEVER score n=1 again.
26	
27	## 2. THE THREE CAUSES OF THE GAP (all measured, none disputed)
28	**A. THE JUDGE CANNOT SEE A SINGLE ONE OF OUR SOURCES.**
29	RACE runs an LLM cleaner that deletes every `[n]` marker and the entire reference list BEFORE judging.
30	MEASURED on 12 real facts x 5 citation formats through the PRODUCTION cleaner:
31	| format | authors survive | journals |
32	|---|---|---|
33	| `[1]` markers (**WHAT WE DO — all 240 of them**) | **0/12** | 0/12 |
34	| `(Acemoglu & Restrepo, 2019)` — the "obvious" fix | **0/12** | 0/12 |
35	| `Acemoglu and Restrepo (2019) show...` | 5/12 | 0/12 |
36	| **`Writing in the JEP in 2019, Acemoglu and Restrepo show...`** | **10/12** | **12/12** |
37	**It is the JOURNAL NAME that anchors the clause as prose. And the year MUST be prose — every one of
38	cellcog's 281 parenthetical `(YYYY)` is deleted.** What the judge reads from us today:
39	*"About 47 percent of total US employment is at risk of computerisation."* — a naked, unattributed
40	assertion. That is Frey & Osborne, 5,223 citations, peer-reviewed. **We did the scholarly work and hid
41	every trace of it.** On a task whose instruction is *"only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles."*
42	
43	**B. WE CANNOT REASON ACROSS SOURCES — BY CONSTRUCTION.**
44	`entailment_judge.py:588` NEUTRAL clause deletes any sentence introducing *"a fact, entity, MECHANISM ... NOT
45	present in the SPAN"*. A cross-source inference IS that. **98% of our sentences are bare facts; bodhi's are ~45%
46	analysis; cellcog's synthesis prose is most of the document.** INSIGHT is 0.32 — the heaviest weight — and our worst.
47	
48	**C. OUR REPORT IS PHYSICALLY UNREADABLE.**
49	12 paragraphs, **median 677 words**, ZERO H3. Every other system on the board — *including the two we beat* —
50	sits at 38-170 words/paragraph. **Our readability (0.3774) is the WORST SCORE ON THE BOARD.** And the judge scores
51	all ~25 criteria in ONE call, writing a comparative analysis BEFORE the numbers => **cross-dimension bleed is real:
52	our wall-of-text impression taxes the other 86% of the score.**
53	
54	## 3. **THE FAITHFULNESS LINE — CORRECTED. THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT SECTION.**
55	I previously told the operator there was a hard trade-off: *"bodhi wins by writing sentences no single source
56	supports; we cannot match that without fabricating."* **THAT WAS WRONG.** Look at what bodhi actually writes:
57	
58	> *"...aggregate impacts were too small to detect over the period studied **[3]** — a pattern consistent with GPT
59	> diffusion lags and within-firm reorganization preceding macro reallocation **[1]**."*
60	
61	**IT CITES TWO SOURCES.** [3] is the empirical null. [1] is the GPT/productivity-paradox paper **WHICH STATES THE
62	DIFFUSION-LAG MECHANISM**. **bodhi is not inventing a mechanism. It is citing a THEORY paper for the mechanism and
63	an EMPIRICAL paper for the finding, and connecting them. THAT IS NOT HALLUCINATION — THAT IS WHAT A LITERATURE
64	REVIEW IS.**
65	
66	Our gate kills that sentence for a stupid reason: it takes the UNION of the cited spans and asks *"is every
67	assertion literally present?"* The composite claim is verbatim in neither — **even though BOTH ITS COMPONENTS ARE
68	FULLY GROUNDED IN THE TWO PAPERS IT CITES.**
69	
70	**THE CORRECTED LINE — design to this:**
71	- **FABRICATING A FACT** — a number, a study, a finding, an attribution that does not exist. **FRAUD. NEVER.**
72	- **CONNECTING GROUNDED CLAIMS** — a finding from paper A + a mechanism stated by paper B, with BOTH CITED.
73	  **SCHOLARSHIP. ALWAYS ALLOWED.**
74	**A mechanism may be asserted if ANY source in the corpus states it AND we cite that source.** (The contract at
75	`scripts/synthesis_contract.py` currently requires the mechanism to be stated by the two papers being compared —
76	**too strict, it forbids exactly bodhi's winning move. FIX IT.**)
77	**STILL DELETED, DETERMINISTICALLY:** any number not in a source | any study/organisation that does not exist |
78	any attribution to someone who did not say it | any mechanism NO paper anywhere states | forecasts | universals.
79	
80	**DROP ANY OTHER RULE THAT COSTS US SCORE AND BUYS US NOTHING.** The operator's instruction is explicit. Audit the
81	pipeline for rules that are pure cost: the 15-sources-per-section rule (unsatisfiable, silently padded), the
82	tier gate, the `[n]` marker convention itself (invisible to the judge — the bibliography can STAY for provenance,
83	but the PROSE must carry the attribution).
84	
85	## 4. THE CORPUS IS THE OTHER HALF — AND OUR RETRIEVAL IS AIMED WRONG
86	MEASURED: `data/cp4_corpus_s3gear_329.corrected.json` = **997 evidence rows, 919 distinct URLs, 530 domains,
87	206 DOIs, 107 journals — and EVERY row already carries a direct quote.** But:
88	- **only 5 rows had AUTHOR NAMES** (which is why in-prose attribution was impossible);
89	- after Crossref enrichment, **120 distinct journal articles — of which only 17 ARE ON-TOPIC.** The rest is
90	  **ResNet, the BMJ PRISMA checklist, Cognitive Psychology 1974 (reading automaticity), Journal of Finance 1996.**
91	  **Prestigious, peer-reviewed, and completely irrelevant.**
92	- Top domains retrieved: researchgate.net (57), bls.gov (29), oecd.org (20), ssrn (12), nber (12) — **grey literature.**
93	**WE DID NOT HAVE A CORPUS PROBLEM. WE HAD AN AIMING PROBLEM.**
94	
95	**cellcog has ~98 ON-TOPIC canonical journal articles. We have 17 (+32 I hand-built tonight by citation-graph
96	expansion) = ~45. THAT IS NOT ENOUGH AND WE MUST NOT SETTLE FOR IT.**
97	
98	**WE HAVE AN AGENTIC RETRIEVAL LOOP WE NEVER USED FOR THIS.** (`PG_AGENTIC_*`: max 15 rounds, 120 queries,
99	convergence on theme saturation + URL overlap; `outline_agent`; the whole query-gen -> search -> fetch -> select ->
100	weight -> dedup -> consolidate chain.) **I spent hours doing MANUAL API pulls while the system's own search agent
101	sat idle. Fix the retrieval AT THE ROOT and run it to SATURATION against a COVERAGE MATRIX, not to a number I picked.**
102	
103	PROVEN TONIGHT: **keyword search cannot find this literature.** Autor-Levy-Murnane (2003, QJE, 4,743 cites) is the
104	most important paper in the field and its title — *"The Skill Content of Recent Technological Change"* — contains
105	**neither "AI" nor "labor market"**. No keyword query finds it. **CITATION-GRAPH EXPANSION does.** Build that in.
106	
107	## 5. WHAT #1 LOOKS LIKE (measured from cellcog's own task-72 artifact)
108	| | cellcog (#1) | POLARIS today |
109	|---|---|---|
110	| on-topic journal sources | ~98 | 22 |
111	| body words | 13,580 | 7,742 |
112	| structure | 9 H2 + **31 H3 + 8 H4** | 11 H2, **0 H3** |
113	| median paragraph | ~100w | **677w** |
114	| in-prose attributions | **133 author-year + 65 journal names** | **~0 visible** |
115	| epistemic labels | **14** (`[Established finding]`) — **THE ONLY SYSTEM ON THE BOARD THAT DOES THIS** | none |
116	| structured abstract | Objective / Methods / Findings / Contributions — **survives the cleaner in full** | none |
117	| **"Scope, Methods, and Source Selection" section** | **narrates its journal-only criteria IN PROSE — survives the cleaner 100% — it EXPLAINS ITS COMPLIANCE TO THE GRADER**, even pre-empting the working-paper objection | **we say nothing** |
118	| analysis vs reporting | ~half analysis | **2%** |
119	| `[n]` markers | 0 | 240 |
120	**"4IR" secretly means "COMPARE AI TO THE PREVIOUS THREE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTIONS"** — ~11.5% of the score across
121	three criteria. We drop it after paragraph one.
122	**Our report OPENS with meta-commentary about itself** (*"This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence
123	on the question above"* — there is no question above) — **and the judge reads the opening and closing most carefully.**
124	
125	## 6. **HOW WE BEAT cellcog, NOT JUST MATCH IT**
126	cellcog's claims are ASSERTED. **OURS CAN BE VERIFIED — every sentence traceable to a span in a real paper.**
127	The rubric explicitly grades **"Data and Factual Support: provides sufficient data, facts, cases, or evidence to
128	support its arguments and analysis"** (comprehensiveness) and **"Depth and Representativeness of Literature
129	Synthesized"**. **That is a criterion we can OUT-SCORE cellcog on, not merely match.**
130	**THE PLAY: cellcog's document architecture + cellcog's adjudication + DEEPER EVIDENCE THAN cellcog + grounding
131	cellcog does not have.** That is a #1 play. Copying bodhi never was.
132	
133	## 7. WHAT IS ALREADY BUILT (reuse it; fix what is wrong)
134	- `scripts/corpus_audit.py` / `enrich_bibliography.py` / `corpus_enrich_merge.py` — Crossref enrichment (works).
135	- `scripts/journal_corpus_build.py` — citation-graph expansion from canonical anchors (works; **this is how you
136	  find the real literature**).
137	- `scripts/journal_corpus_fetch.py` + `deep_fetch.py` — Crossref abstracts + Unpaywall OA full text.
138	- `scripts/cleaner_survival_test.py` — **proved which citation format survives. Reuse the finding.**
139	- `scripts/synthesis_contract.py` — typed adjudication, 14/14, **ZERO false admissions. But its mechanism rule is
140	  TOO STRICT (see §3) — fix it to allow a mechanism stated by ANY cited source.**
141	- `scripts/cellcog_composer.py` — first cut of the composer (evidence cards -> attributed, adjudicated prose).
142	- Baseline pinned: rank10 = **0.4382** (k=5, SD 0.0074).
143	
144	## 8. YOUR TASK — ONE SINGLE, COMPLETE, EXECUTABLE PLAN TO **BEAT 0.5578**
145	Cover, end to end:
146	1. **RETRIEVAL AT THE ROOT** — query generation, search, fetch, select, weight, dedup, consolidate. Journal-first.
147	   Citation-graph expansion. A COVERAGE MATRIX as the stopping condition (every restructuring dimension and every
148	   industry the task names). How many papers, and how do we know when we have enough?
149	2. **THE EVIDENCE LAYER** — cards with verbatim spans + declared fields (level/horizon/method/mechanism), canonical
150	   dedup, quality weighting.
151	3. **THE SYNTHESIS LANE** — the corrected faithfulness line. Typed adjudication. What EXACTLY may a sentence assert?
152	4. **THE COMPOSER** — cellcog's architecture: H2/H3/H4, ~100w paragraphs, in-prose journal-named attribution,
153	   epistemic labels, structured abstract, Scope-and-Methods section, 4IR as an organising spine, industry coverage.
154	5. **WHAT OLD RULES TO DROP** — name them, with the evidence that they cost score and buy nothing.
155	6. **THE WHEEL** — fix -> compose -> score k=5 -> read the prose line by line -> fix again. What is the loop, and
156	   what is the stopping condition?
157	7. **HONEST CEILING + KILL RULES.** If your plan does not beat 0.5578, say so and name exactly what is missing.
158	
159	Ground every lever in a VERBATIM rubric criterion or a MEASURED artifact fact. Where uncertain, SEARCH (papers,
160	GitHub, and the artifacts at `/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/`).
161	**No proxies. We lost an entire night to those.**
162

7	- [w=0.10] Grounding in AI and the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Assesses whether the review adequately defines AI within the context of the 4IR and explains its fundamental role as a driver of labor market restructuring. This contextual framing is crucial for understanding the scope and nature of the impacts discussed.
8	- [w=0.25] Breadth of Labor Market Restructuring Dimensions Covered :: Evaluates the extent to which the review addresses the multifaceted nature of AI's impact on the labor market, including job creation, job displacement, job transformation, changes in skill demands, wage effects, and productivity changes.
9	- [w=0.25] Scope of Industry-Specific Analysis :: Checks if the review examines the restructuring impact of AI across a diverse range of relevant industries, highlighting both common trends and sector-specific nuances. The task explicitly requires focusing on 'various industries.'
10	- [w=0.15] Exploration of AI's Disruptive Character and Scale :: Assesses whether the review sufficiently explores the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI, including the magnitude, speed, and transformative potential of these changes on labor market structures and dynamics.
11	- [w=0.15] Depth and Representativeness of Literature Synthesized :: Evaluates if the review synthesizes a broad and representative selection of current, high-quality academic literature (from the specified journal articles), covering the main research themes, findings, and ongoing debates regarding AI's impact on the labor market. This ensures the review's content is built upon a solid and comprehensive foundation of existing knowledge.
12	- [w=0.10] Balanced Discussion of AI's Labor Market Impacts :: Assesses whether the review presents a nuanced perspective by discussing both the challenges (e.g., displacement, skill gaps, inequality) and opportunities (e.g., new job creation, productivity gains, enhanced work quality) arising from AI's influence on the labor market.
13	
14	=== INSIGHT (weight 0.32) ===
15	- [w=0.25] Analytical Depth in Characterizing AI-Driven Labor Market Restructuring Mechanisms :: Assesses if the review moves beyond superficial descriptions of labor market changes to deeply analyze the underlying *mechanisms* (e.g., task automation, skill augmentation, job creation/destruction dynamics, organizational adaptation) through which AI specifically reshapes job roles, skill requirements, and overall labor market structures.
16	- [w=0.25] Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation of AI's Disruptive Impacts Across Industries :: Evaluates the review's ability to critically synthesize diverse findings from high-quality journal articles to present a nuanced understanding of AI's 'significant disruptions.' This includes identifying patterns of impact, variations across different industries, and areas of consensus, debate, or uncertainty in the literature, rather than merely cataloging effects.
17	- [w=0.15] Insightful Integration of AI's Role within the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Measures how effectively the review articulates and leverages the 4IR framework to provide a deeper, more contextualized understanding of the *nature, scale, and interconnectedness* of AI's transformative power on the labor market, rather than treating AI's impact in isolation or merely mentioning the 4IR superficially.
18	- [w=0.20] Identification and Articulation of Emergent Themes, Theoretical Linkages, or Novel Perspectives :: Assesses whether the review transcends simple summarization to identify and clearly articulate overarching emergent themes, significant theoretical linkages between concepts, or potentially novel perspectives derived from the synthesis of the reviewed literature on AI's labor market impact.
19	- [w=0.15] Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas :: Evaluates the review's capacity to distill strategically valuable implications (e.g., for policy, education, workforce adaptation strategies) and to clearly delineate critical research gaps or promising future research agendas based on the comprehensive synthesis of the current state of knowledge.
20	
21	=== INSTRUCTION_FOLLOWING (weight 0.25) ===
22	- [w=0.10] Adherence to 'Literature Review' Format and Purpose :: Assesses if the article is structured and executed as a literature review, meaning it synthesizes and discusses existing published research on the topic, rather than presenting new empirical findings or being solely an opinion piece.
23	- [w=0.20] Consistent Focus on 'Restructuring Impact of AI on the Labor Market' :: Evaluates whether the article's content remains consistently and primarily focused on AI's restructuring effects on the labor market, as per the core task instruction, without significant digressions into unrelated AI applications or general economic theory not tied to labor impacts.
24	- [w=0.15] Integration of 'AI as a Key Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' Theme :: Checks if the review explicitly incorporates and discusses AI within the specific context of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, as mandated by the task's focus requirements.
25	- [w=0.15] Explicit Addressal of AI-Driven 'Significant Disruptions' in the Labor Market :: Assesses whether the review specifically addresses and elaborates on the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI in the labor market, fulfilling another key focus point of the task.
26	- [w=0.15] Coverage of AI's Impact on 'Various Industries' :: Evaluates if the review discusses the impact of AI on the labor market across a spectrum of 'various industries,' rather than limiting its scope to a single industry or providing only a generic overview, as per the instruction.
27	- [w=0.15] Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles' :: Verifies that all cited sources are academic, peer-reviewed journal articles (or meet a clear standard of 'high-quality' if defined contextually, e.g., leading journals), and that other forms of literature (e.g., books, conference proceedings, news articles, blogs, non-peer-reviewed reports) are not cited, adhering to the 'only cites high-quality... journal articles' constraint.
28	- [w=0.10] Exclusive Citation of 'English-Language' Journal Articles :: Ensures that all journal articles cited in the review are originally published in English or are established English translations, strictly adhering to the language constraint for sources.
29	
30	=== READABILITY (weight 0.14) ===
31	- [w=0.20] L1: Language Clarity, Precision, and Academic Tone :: Evaluates the accuracy and fluency of sentence construction, grammar, spelling, and punctuation. Assesses the precise, consistent, and appropriate use of technical terminology related to AI, labor markets, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and specific industries. Ensures an objective, formal academic tone suitable for a literature review. For example, terms like 'skill-biased technical change' or specific AI techniques should be used correctly and, if necessary, briefly contextualized for a broader academic audience.
32	- [w=0.20] S1: Overall Structure and Logical Organization :: Assesses the coherence and logic of the review's macro-structure. This includes a clear introduction defining the review's scope, purpose, and organizational map; logically sequenced thematic sections with informative headings that reflect the facets of AI's impact on labor (e.g., by industry, skill level, type of disruption); and a conclusion that effectively synthesizes the main themes from the literature and perhaps points to gaps or future research directions.
33	- [w=0.15] S2: Paragraph Cohesion and Transitions :: Evaluates if each paragraph focuses on a single, clear idea or theme derived from the literature, with sentences flowing logically within the paragraph. Assesses the effectiveness of transitional phrases, sentences, and logical connectors in linking ideas smoothly between paragraphs and across different sections of the review, ensuring a coherent narrative thread.
34	- [w=0.15] P1: Clarity and Synthesis in Presenting Sourced Information :: Assesses how clearly, concisely, and accurately key arguments, findings, theories, and methodologies from the cited journal articles are presented. Evaluates the effectiveness of synthesizing information from multiple sources to build a coherent understanding of topics (e.g., AI's effect on job displacement vs. creation, or changes in skill demands across industries), rather than just serially summarizing individual papers. Checks for appropriate information density and avoidance of redundancy.
35	- [w=0.10] D1: Clarity of Data/Evidence Referenced or Summarized :: Evaluates the clarity with which quantitative or qualitative data, empirical findings, or specific evidence from cited studies (e.g., statistics on automation rates, survey results on worker sentiment) are described, interpreted, or summarized within the review. If the review itself uses tables or figures to compile or synthesize information from sources (e.g., a summary table of key studies and their findings), assesses their clarity, labeling, and effectiveness in aiding comprehension.
36	- [w=0.10] F1: Formatting, Layout, and Visual Consistency :: Evaluates the use of consistent and professional formatting (e.g., font choice and size, spacing, margins, heading styles) that enhances readability. Assesses the overall visual layout for ease of navigation and reduced reader fatigue, including clear paragraph breaks. Also considers the visual consistency and unobtrusive integration of in-text citations and the clarity of the bibliography's formatting, which contributes to the academic presentation.
37	- [w=0.10] A1: Audience Adaptation and Explanation of Terms :: Assesses if the language, level of detail, and style of argumentation are appropriate for an academic audience interested in AI's impact on the labor market. Evaluates whether potentially ambiguous or highly specialized terms are sufficiently contextualized or briefly explained to ensure understanding by readers who may not be experts in every specific sub-domain discussed (e.g., specific AI algorithms or niche economic theories).

python3 - <<'EOF'
import json,re,statistics
def load(f):
    for l in open(f):
        d=json.loads(l)
        if str(d['id'])=='72': return d['article']
sys=['cellcog-max','bodhi','WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612','lunon','gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch','openai-deepresearch']
for s in sys:
    a=load(s+'.jsonl')
    if not a: continue
    lines=a.split('\n')
    h1=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^# [^#]',l)]
    h2=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^## [^#]',l)]
    h3=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^### [^#]',l)]
    h4=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^#### ',l)]
    paras=[p for p in re.split(r'\n\s*\n',a) if p.strip() and not p.strip().startswith('#') and not p.strip().startswith('|') and not p.strip().startswith('-') and not p.strip().startswith('*')]
    pw=[len(p.split()) for p in paras]
    tables=a.count('\n|')
    tabrows = len([l for l in lines if l.strip().startswith('|')])
    bullets=len([l for l in lines if re.match(r'^\s*[-*] ',l)])
    # citation formats
    brack=len(re.findall(r'\[\d+\]',a))
    parens=len(re.findall(r'\([A-Z][A-Za-z\-]+(?: (?:&|and|et al\.?) [A-Z][A-Za-z\-]+)*,? \d{4}[a-z]?\)',a))
    yearparen=len(re.findall(r'\(\d{4}[a-z]?\)',a))
    print(f"{s:<32} words={len(a.split()):>6} H1={len(h1)} H2={len(h2):>2} H3={len(h3):>2} H4={len(h4):>2} paras={len(paras):>3} medPara={statistics.median(pw) if pw else 0:>5.0f} maxPara={max(pw) if pw else 0:>4} tableRows={tabrows:>3} bullets={bullets:>3} [n]={brack:>3} (Auth,YYYY)={parens:>3} Auth(YYYY)={yearparen:>3}")
EOF

# The Restructuring Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labor Market in the Fourth Industrial Revolution: A Literature Review

Artificial intelligence is restructuring labor markets not by destroying a fixed stock of jobs but by **recomposing the task content of work, fracturing the link between skill and wage, and redistributing returns toward a narrow stratum of complementary assets.** The empirical record is strikingly unsettled: across published models, the share of US occupations judged "highly exposed" to AI ranges from under 3% to more than 51% — a 3.6-fold divergence in average scores that means the field cannot yet agree on the size of the phenomenon it studies[Michelle Yin exposure-measure instability](https://michelleyin.org/pub-ai-measurement.html).

# The Restructuring Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labor Market: A Peer-Reviewed Literature Review

## Abstract

**Objective.** This review synthesizes peer-reviewed evidence on the restructuring impact of artificial intelligence on labor markets, situating AI as the signature technology of the Fourth Industrial Revolution and asking what the literature supports about its effects on employment, wages, tasks, the labor share, and labor-process autonomy.

**Methods.** The review draws on approximately 96 English-language peer-reviewed journal articles from economics, management and operations, sociology and technology studies, and applied health and education venues, spanning the early 1990s through April 2026. Evidence is organized into three technology eras — computerization and polarization, industrial robots and narrow AI, and generative AI — with sectoral heterogeneity and cross-cutting analyses nested within.

**Findings.** Heterogeneity, not displacement, is the central peer-reviewed finding. Recent meta-analyses report average robot–labor-market associations close to zero, though individual causal studies (notably Acemoglu and Restrepo, 2020) identify significant local effects. Institutional mediation proves decisive across five levels — platform, firm, regional, national, and informal-sector — determining how exposure translates into realized outcomes. The generative-AI era shows augmentation within firms alongside displacement on open platforms, while exposure measurement itself is methodologically contested.

**Contributions.** The review develops eight original syntheses, each epistemically tagged by evidentiary status: the inversion of exposure from routine to cognitive work; the freelance–population paradox; augmentation as skill compression; the autonomy–employment distinction; the Turing Trap as endogenous technology choice; the jagged frontier and the limits of exposure measurement; the gender exposure reversal; and the Global South's informality buffer as an institutional analogue to European coordinated-bargaining systems.

---

=== SCOPE/METHODS SECTION ===
### 1.2 Scope, Methods, and Source Selection

This review is restricted by design to peer-reviewed, English-language journal articles. The corpus was assembled through iterative database searches across economics journals (*Quarterly Journal of Economics*, *American Economic Review*, *Journal of Political Economy*, *Journal of Labor Economics*, *Journal of Economic Perspectives*, *European Economic Review*, *Review of Economic Dynamics*, *Structural Change and Economic Dynamics*), management and operations journals (*Management Science*, *Organization Science*, *International Journal of Production Economics*, *Industry and Innovation*, *Strategic Management Journal*), sociology and technology-studies journals (*Work, Employment and Society*, *New Technology, Work and Employment*, *Organization Studies*, *Human Relations*, *Technological Forecasting and Social Change*, *Technology in Society*, *Social Forces*), and applied health and education journals (*JAMA Network Open*, *Cogent Education*) where sector-specific evidence is relevant. Approximately ninety-six articles comprise the core evidence base.

The temporal scope spans the early 1990s through April 2026, tracing the theoretical evolution from skill-biased technological change through the routine-biased reconceptualization to the task-based framework that dominates contemporary analysis. Empirical emphasis falls on the 2018–2026 window, during which the most rigorous causal studies of industrial robots, narrow AI, and generative AI have been published.

Three epistemic caveats attach to the peer-reviewed constraint. First, publication bias is not merely hypothetical: Guarascio et al. (2025) and Jurkat et al. (2025) both document preferential selection of negative wage results in U.S.-focused robot studies. Second, high-quality working papers — notably Webb's 2019 patent-exposure measure — are excluded even where they have shaped the field; where essential, we reference them as contextual rather than primary evidence. Third, the peer-review process privileges strong causal identification, which systematically underweights qualitative, ethnographic, and descriptive cross-country work. We compensate by drawing explicitly on labor-process scholarship in sociology and industrial relations journals, where methodological norms favor ethnography and case analysis.



=== INSIGHT SECTION 5.1-5.2 ===
## 5. Contested Debates and Novel Insights

The heterogeneous evidence assembled in Sections 3 and 4 invites syntheses that no single paper in the peer-reviewed literature articulates. This section develops eight such syntheses. Each is epistemically tagged to distinguish what the evidence has established from what it suggests and what we propose as analytical hypothesis: *[Established Finding]* for claims supported by multiple replicated peer-reviewed studies; *[Emerging Pattern]* for findings consistent across recent evidence but lacking long-horizon confirmation; *[Conceptual Argument]* for theoretical claims whose empirical test remains underdeveloped; and *[Analytical Hypothesis / Our Synthesis]* for extrapolations we introduce rather than find. The convention is not cosmetic: a PhD-grade literature review must distinguish what the field knows from what the field has begun to suggest, and from what we ourselves argue.

### 5.1 The Inversion of Exposure: From Routine to Cognitive

*[Emerging Pattern]*

The routine-biased technological change framework (Autor, Levy, & Murnane, 2003) positioned non-routine cognitive tasks — analytical problem-solving, complex communication, interpersonal judgment — as the "safe harbor" from automation. The empirical architecture of the past two decades depended on this taxonomy: middle-wage routine jobs hollowed out while non-routine cognitive work at the top and non-routine manual work at the bottom expanded. Generative AI inverts this pattern. Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin, and Rock's (2024) exposure rankings place text-intensive cognitive occupations — writers, translators, proofreaders, mathematicians, public relations specialists, accountants, legal secretaries — at the top. Demireva's (2026) commercial-deployment exposure measure converges: information-processing and communication-heavy jobs are most affected.

The implication extends beyond empirical update. The routine/non-routine axis was built on the assumption that non-routine tasks resisted codification. Large language models demonstrate that many cognitive tasks previously considered non-codifiable are, in fact, pattern-learnable from large text corpora — not because those tasks are mechanical but because the relevant patterns are present in the data on which models train. The taxonomy therefore requires revision. A more defensible axis for the generative-AI era may be *pattern-predictable from training corpora* versus *requiring embodied experience or contextual integration*. This is a distinct analytical partition from routine versus non-routine, and it cuts across the earlier taxonomy in ways the peer-reviewed literature is only beginning to work out.

### 5.2 The Freelance-Population Paradox

*[Established Finding + Our Synthesis]*

Two well-identified findings sit in apparent tension. Platform-level evidence documents substantial realized displacement: Hui, Reshef, and Zhou (2024), Demirer, Hannane, and Zhu (2025), and Teutloff and colleagues (2025) report demand contractions of seventeen to twenty-four percent in exposed freelance clusters within months of ChatGPT's release. Population-level evidence shows no detectable effect: Kauhanen and Rouvinen's (2025) Finnish analysis, covering all wage earners for two years, finds no statistically significant wage or employment differences between more- and less-exposed occupations.

Our synthesis is that these findings are not in tension; they document institutional mediation at work. Platforms lack internal labor markets, employment protection, notice periods, and the within-firm redeployment channels documented in Dauth, Findeisen, Suedekum, and Woessner's (2021) German data. Freelance platforms are the closest empirical approximation to unconstrained labor-market adjustment available — what they reveal is what national labor markets *would* transmit without institutional buffers. The policy implication is direct: labor-market-flexibility advocates who argue for weakening employment protection to accelerate reallocation are arguing, in effect, for moving aggregate labor markets closer to the freelance-platform baseline. The peer-reviewed evidence on what that baseline looks like under generative-AI exposure suggests the costs of such reallocation would be borne quickly and disproportionately by exposed workers.

content.text:
I have everything I need. Verified: the rubric verbatim, the RACE formula (`target/(target+reference)`, reference = gemini-2.5-pro-DR, target is always `article_1`), the cleaner prompt, all 9 rivals' task-72 scores and document geometry, and our own corpus/composer state.

---

# ONE PLAN: BEAT 0.5578

## 0. The arithmetic that stops the bar from being lowered

`deepresearch_bench_race.py:160`: **`overall = target_total / (target_total + reference_total)`**. Reference = gemini-2.5-pro-DR. So a score is a *ratio to gemini*, and the required uplift is computable exactly:

| dim | weight | ours | ratio-to-ref now | needed (0.5578) | **required uplift** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **INSIGHT** | **0.32** | 0.4238 | 0.735× | 1.330× | **1.81×** |
| COMPREHENSIVENESS | 0.29 | 0.4549 | 0.835× | 1.291× | 1.55× |
| INSTRUCTION-FOLLOWING | 0.25 | 0.4409 | 0.789× | 1.237× | 1.57× |
| READABILITY | 0.14 | 0.3774 | 0.607× | 1.081× | 1.78× |
| **OVERALL** | | **0.4382** | **0.780×** | **1.261×** | **1.617×** |

**We must produce 1.62× the raw judge points we produce today.** INSIGHT needs the *largest* uplift (1.81×) *and* carries the *heaviest* weight. That is the crux — not readability, not citations. Any plan that does not have an answer for INSIGHT is a bodhi-class plan.

**Three corrections to the brief before we build on it:**
1. **§6 is wrong.** "Data and Factual Support" is **not in task 72's rubric** (verified: `grep` on `/home/polaris/polaris_project/race_task72_criteria.txt` → not present). It's a generic RACE criterion from another task. The real out-scoring surfaces are **COMP-5 "Depth and Representativeness of Literature Synthesized"** (w=0.15) and **READ-D1** which *verbatim* rewards tables. Don't build on a criterion that doesn't exist.
2. **The citation-format lever is real but bounded.** bodhi is the best *known* task-72 score (0.5441) with **105 `[n]` markers and zero in-prose attribution**. So the cleaner-survival finding cannot be the engine of #1. It buys IF-6 (0.0375) + IF-7 (0.025) + READ-D1/F1 ≈ **8% of score**, no more. Ship it, don't lean on it.
3. **The corpus is smaller than the brief says.** `outputs/journal_corpus_content.json`: **70 papers, but only 12 with full text and 29 with abstracts.** `evidence_cards.json`: 133 cards from **32 distinct DOIs**. Our *effective* corpus is **32 papers**, not 45 and not 70. **Papers-with-extractable-text is the binding constraint of this entire plan.**

---

## 1. THE STOPPING CONDITION: a coverage matrix read straight off the rubric

Not a paper count I picked. Every cell is a verbatim rubric phrase.

- **MATRIX A — restructuring dimensions** (COMP-2, w=0.25, names them verbatim): job creation | job displacement | job transformation | skill demand | wage effects | productivity. × {causal, descriptive, theoretical}. **18 cells, ≥2 spanned papers each.**
- **MATRIX B — industries** (COMP-3 w=0.25 + IF-5 w=0.15 = **11% of score**): manufacturing, healthcare, finance/banking, retail, transport/logistics, legal/professional services, education, software/IT, creative & media, customer service/BPO, agriculture, public sector. **12 cells, ≥2 papers each, each with a *sector-specific nuance*, not a generic sentence.**
- **MATRIX C — mechanisms** (INS-1, w=0.25, names them verbatim): task automation | skill augmentation | job creation/destruction dynamics | organizational adaptation. **4 cells, ≥3 papers each — and these must include THEORY papers, which are the *supply* of citable mechanisms (see §3).**
- **MATRIX D — the 4IR spine** (COMP-1 + INS-3 + IF-3 = **11.5% of score**): the three prior industrial revolutions, ≥1 journal paper each (economic-history literature: Mokyr/Vickers/Ziebarth *JEP* 2015; Bessen; Frey). **We currently have zero.**
- **MATRIX E — geography**: US | Europe | China | Global South. 4 cells. (This is where cellcog mined a novel insight — "the Global South's informality buffer".)
- **MATRIX F — balance** (COMP-6): every A-cell must have ≥1 *opportunity* card and ≥1 *challenge* card.

**Saturation = every cell has ≥2 papers with a VERBATIM SPAN.** Estimated distinct papers: **110–130 with extractable text.** Today: **32.** That is the honest gap.

---

## 2. RETRIEVAL AT THE ROOT — four engines, one canonical store

**Backbone: OpenAlex** (free, no key, exposes `referenced_works`, `cited_by_api_url`, `best_oa_location`, `concepts`) + Crossref for metadata + Unpaywall for OA PDFs.

**R1 — Citation-graph expansion (primary; `scripts/journal_corpus_build.py` already does this).** Seed ~25 canonical anchors (ALM *QJE* 2003; Acemoglu–Restrepo *JPE* 2020/*AER* 2018/*JEP* 2019; Autor–Dorn *AER* 2013; Goos–Manning–Salomons *AER* 2014; Frey–Osborne *TFSC* 2017; Brynjolfsson–Mitchell *Science* 2017; Eloundou et al. *Science* 2024; Brynjolfsson–Li–Raymond *QJE* 2025; Noy–Zhang *Science* 2023; Agrawal–Gans–Goldfarb *JEP* 2019; Graetz–Michaels *REStat* 2018; Dauth et al. *JEEA* 2021; Felten–Raj–Seamans *SMJ* 2021; Mokyr–Vickers–Ziebarth *JEP* 2015; Makridakis *Futures* 2017…). Expand **backward (references) AND forward (citations), 2 hops.** Rank by `log1p(citations) × venue_tier × co-citation-with-≥2-anchors × embedding-sim-to-rubric-cell`. **This is the only method that finds ALM-2003** — its title contains neither "AI" nor "labor market". Proven.
**R1b — Concept-ID queries**, not keywords: query OpenAlex by concept (e.g. "skill-biased technological change", "routine-biased technical change", "task-based framework") rather than by string. Keyword search structurally cannot find this literature; concept IDs can.

**R2 — The agentic loop we never pointed at this** (`PG_AGENTIC_*`: `MAX_ROUNDS`, `MAX_QUERIES`, `CONVERGENCE_THEME_SATURATION`, `CONVERGENCE_URL_OVERLAP` — all already wired). **The one change: the query generator emits one query family per EMPTY MATRIX CELL, and `CONVERGENCE_THEME_SATURATION` is measured against the matrix, not against a generic theme set.** That is what turns "15 rounds" into "run until the rubric is covered."

**R3 — Venue sweep.** For each of ~30 whitelisted journals (QJE, AER, JPE, JOLE, JEP, REStat, EJ, JEEA, RAND, *ILR Review*, *Work Employment & Society*, *New Technology Work & Employment*, *Research Policy*, *TFSC*, *Technology in Society*, *Management Science*, *Organization Science*, *ISR*, *MISQ*, *SMJ*, *JBR*, *Science*, *Nature Human Behaviour*, *PNAS*, *JAMA Netw Open*, *IJPE*, *Industry & Innovation*, *Human Relations*, *Socio-Economic Review*), pull AI×labor papers 2015-2026 by citation count. **This makes IF-6 compliance true by construction rather than by hope.**

**SELECT (hard gate).** `type == journal-article` ∧ `ISSN present` ∧ `language == en`. **Working papers (NBER, SSRN), preprints (arXiv), reports (OECD, WEF, McKinsey), and statistics pages (bls.gov) are EXCLUDED FROM THE CITABLE CORPUS.** IF-6 names them *verbatim*: *"other forms of literature (e.g., books, conference proceedings, news articles, blogs, **non-peer-reviewed reports**) are not cited."* They survive only in a **background store used to steer search, never to cite.** This single gate deletes our researchgate(57)/bls(29)/oecd(20)/ssrn(12)/nber(12) problem.
**Published-version resolver:** every candidate is resolved to its journal-of-record DOI via Crossref title match. We cite the *QJE* version, not the NBER WP.

**WEIGHT.** `w = log1p(citations) × venue_tier × recency × design_strength` (RCT/quasi-experiment 1.0, meta-analysis 1.1, structural 0.8, theory 0.7, descriptive 0.6). Weight decides which paper anchors a contested claim and drives the `RANK_EVIDENCE` synthesis operator. **Tier is a WEIGHT, never a FILTER** (see §5).

**DEDUP.** Canonical key = DOI; fallback `normalize(title)+first_author+year`. WP↔journal duplicates merge into the journal record.

**CONSOLIDATE — this is where we have actually been failing.** Per paper: OpenAlex `best_oa_location` → PDF → GROBID/pdfminer → sections; else Crossref abstract; else Semantic Scholar abstract; else publisher landing page. **A paper with no extractable text is NOT IN THE CORPUS.** It cannot produce a span, so it cannot be cited — and citing what we cannot quote is precisely the fabrication risk. **When two papers fill the same cell, prefer the OA one.** Full text yields ~10 cards; an abstract yields ~2.
**Target: ≥120 papers with ≥1 extractable span; ≥400 cards.** (Today: 41 / 133.)

## 3. THE EVIDENCE LAYER

Extend the existing card (`outputs/evidence_cards.json` already carries `level/horizon/method/mechanisms` — good):

```
{doi, title, authors[], venue, year, citations, oa,
 span,            # VERBATIM ≤60w + char offsets
 claim,           # one-sentence restatement
 kind: FINDING | MECHANISM | DEFINITION | METHOD_CRITIQUE | POLICY,   # ← NEW, and it is the unlock
 dimension[], industry[], geography, level, horizon, method,
 effect_direction: +|-|0|mixed,  magnitude:{value,unit}|null,
 mechanisms[],    # literal strings from the span
 attribution,     # "Writing in the Quarterly Journal of Economics in 2003, Autor, Levy, and Murnane show that ..."
 weight}
```

**The fabrication floor, and it never moves:** an LLM proposes the card, then a **deterministic check asserts `span` is a literal substring of the source text** (whitespace-normalised). **Any card that fails is DISCARDED, not repaired.**

## 4. THE SYNTHESIS LANE — the faithfulness line, corrected

The brief's §3 correction is right but **incomplete**. cellcog does something stronger, and it's the reason it's #1. Verbatim from its §5.2:

> *"**Our synthesis is that** these findings are not in tension; they document institutional mediation at work."*

and its §5 header tags every claim: *`[Established Finding]` / `[Emerging Pattern]` / `[Conceptual Argument]` / `[Analytical Hypothesis / Our Synthesis]`* — **cellcog is the only system on the board that does this.**

That is not a hedge. It is a **change of speech act**. "X causes Y" asserts a fact about the world and must be grounded. "*Our synthesis is that* X causes Y" asserts a fact about *our reasoning*, which is true by construction. **It is strictly more honest than what we ship today**, where we assert bare facts and the cleaner strips the citation off them.

So: **three assertion types, three proof obligations.**

| type | may assert | proof obligation | prose marker |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EVIDENCE** | a fact, number, finding, attribution | verbatim span from ONE cited source entails it. **`entailment_judge.py` UNCHANGED.** | in-prose attribution |
| **SYNTHESIS** | a typed relation over ≥2 admitted premises from ≥2 sources; **may name a mechanism iff a `kind:MECHANISM` card from ANY cited source states it** | `synthesis_contract.validate()`, mechanism rule fixed | all sources attributed in prose |
| **ARGUMENT** | the review's own extrapolation | (a) no number, no new entity, no new attribution; (b) every factual component traces to an admitted card; (c) **carried by an explicit epistemic label** | `*[Analytical Hypothesis — our synthesis]*` |

**The exact fix to `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/synthesis_contract.py`:**
Rule 6 (lines 197-205) currently resolves `stated` from `prem` — the premises being *compared*. That is why bodhi's winning sentence dies. Add `Synthesis.mechanism_premise_ids: list[str]` (cards of `kind==MECHANISM`, from **any** source, which **must be cited in the sentence**), and resolve:
```python
stated = {m.lower() for p in (prem + mech_prem) for m in p.mechanisms}
```
bodhi's sentence now passes: premises = [empirical-null card from paper 3], mechanism_premises = [diffusion-lag card from paper 1], both attributed. **Rules 4 (no new number), 5 (no new entity), 2/3 (≥2 admitted sources), 8 (must adjudicate) are untouched — the 14/14 zero-false-admission property is preserved,** because the mechanism must still be a literal string from a real paper's span.

**Still deleted, deterministically, forever:** any number not in a span | any study or organisation that does not exist | any attribution to someone who did not say it | **any mechanism no paper anywhere states** | any *empirical* forecast.

## 5. RULES TO DROP — named, with the evidence that each costs score and buys nothing

1. **DROP the `FORECAST` regex** (`synthesis_contract.py:84` — bans `will|by 20[3-9]\d|predict|forecast|future will`). **INS-5, verbatim: *"Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas… clearly delineate critical research gaps or promising future research agendas."*** w=0.15 × 0.32 = **4.8% of total score, made structurally unreachable by our own regex.** → Replace with a narrow rule: forbid only *empirical predictions carrying a number or a date*. **Allow** "future research should…", "the policy implication is…". A research agenda is not a factual claim about the world.
2. **DROP the `UNIVERSAL` regex as written** (bans `all|every|most of|majority|none|no one`). It deletes exactly the meta-language INS-2 (w=0.25 → **8% of score**) pays for: *"areas of consensus, debate, or uncertainty in the literature, rather than merely cataloging effects."* → Replace: universals are illegal **about the world**, legal **about the corpus** — and *checkable*, because we can count our own cards. `"None of the fourteen causal studies in this review finds…"` is a true, verifiable, corpus-level claim. Add a `CORPUS_CLAIM` type validated by counting the card store.
3. **DROP `[n]` markers as the citation mechanism.** MEASURED (`outputs/cleaner_survival.json`): `[1]` markers → **authors 0/12, years 0/12, venues 0/12** survive. The cleaner prompt says verbatim: *"remove all citation links, citation marks (such as [1], [2]…), reference lists, footnotes."* **We ship 240 of them; the judge sees zero.** → **The PROSE carries the attribution.** Format E (`"Writing in the Quarterly Journal of Economics in 2003, Autor, Levy, and Murnane show…"`) survives **authors 10/12, years 10/12, venues 9/12**; format D survives **venues 12/12**. **Rotate D and E.** Keep the References section — it costs nothing (the cleaner returns empty string for a pure-reference chunk).
4. **DROP the 15-sources-per-section rule.** Unsatisfiable at 32 usable papers ⇒ silently padded ⇒ **that is how ResNet, the BMJ PRISMA checklist, and a 1974 paper on reading automaticity got into an AI-labour review.** Padding actively *damages* IF-6 (w=0.15) and COMP-5 (w=0.15). → A section cites as many sources as it has admitted cards for, **minimum 3; a section with <3 is MERGED, not padded.**
5. **DROP the tier gate as a filter.** Under this design, **theory papers are the supply of citable mechanisms** — the thing INS-1 (w=0.25 → **8% of score**, *"deeply analyze the underlying mechanisms"*) pays for. → Tier becomes a *weight*, never an exclusion.
6. **DROP the opening meta-paragraph.** Ours, verbatim: *"This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above."* **There is no question above.** The judge writes its comparative analysis **before** scoring (confirmed in `prompt/score_prompt_en.py`), and it reads the opening first. → Replace with a **structured Abstract (Objective / Methods / Findings / Contributions)**, which survives the cleaner 100% because it contains no citation marks.
7. **DROP grey literature from the citable set.** researchgate(57), bls.gov(29), oecd.org(20), ssrn(12), nber(12). IF-6 names them as violations verbatim. **Search fuel only. Never a citation.**

## 7. THE WHEEL

Each turn (~90 min):
1. **`corpus`** — retrieve to matrix saturation. **Print the matrix and NAME THE EMPTY CELLS.**
2. **`cards`** — extract + verbatim-substring check. Print cards/paper and cards/cell.
3. **`compose`** → `report.md`.
4. **`lint` — a build-FAILING structural preflight**: median para ≤90w · max para ≤160w · ≥30 H3 · ≥4 tables · ≥130 in-prose attributions · **0 `[n]` in body** · ≥12 epistemic labels · ≥10 industries named · abstract present · Scope&Methods present · no meta-opening. **NEVER score a report that fails the linter.** A failed k=5 costs an hour and teaches nothing.
5. **`score` — k=5 paired, always.** Judge SD 0.0074 ⇒ SE = 0.0033 ⇒ **smallest resolvable effect +0.0094. Never score n=1 again.**
6. **`read-cleaned` — run our report through the PRODUCTION cleaner and read what the judge actually reads, line by line.** Every wasted night came from optimising an artifact the judge never saw.
7. **`mine` — read the judge's own per-criterion `analysis` text.** The harness already emits comparative prose and 0-10 scores **for both articles, per criterion**. It is the highest-signal artifact we own and **we have never systematically read it.** The next lever is deterministic: fix the criterion where `(ref_score − our_score) × crit_weight × dim_weight` is largest.

**STOP:** three consecutive k=5 runs with **mean − 2·SE > 0.5578**, i.e. **mean ≥ 0.5644**. Anything less is noise, not a win.

**KILL RULES:**
- Matrix >20% empty after a full corpus run ⇒ the literature doesn't exist at that granularity. **Collapse the cell and convert it into a stated research gap** — an empty cell is INS-5 points.
- Two consecutive iterations moving the mean < +0.0094 ⇒ that lever is exhausted. Move to the next-highest-weighted criterion. **Do not grind.**
- Linter green + matrix saturated + still < 0.5578 ⇒ **the missing thing is corpus depth. The answer is more papers-with-text, not more prose.**

## 8. HONEST CEILING

**Required: 1.617× our current raw judge points.** That is a rebuild, and I will not pretend otherwise.

**What I am confident about.** READABILITY (1.78×) is the largest ratio and the most mechanical: 677-word paragraphs and zero H3, against a board where *every* system sits at 29-71w and 31-50 H3. That is a bug. INSTRUCTION-FOLLOWING (1.57×) is mostly (a) citing grey literature that IF-6 names verbatim as a violation and (b) never stating our compliance — both fixed deterministically. COMPREHENSIVENESS (1.55×) is what the coverage matrix *is*.

**What is genuinely hard: INSIGHT — 1.81× uplift at 0.32 weight.** It needs ~8 original, defensible cross-source syntheses. **And those can only be *found*, not written — the "freelance-vs-population paradox" only exists as an insight if the corpus contains BOTH the platform-displacement studies AND the Finnish population null.** *This is why retrieval is the root and not a preliminary.*

**The one thing that can make this fail, and the number to watch in iteration 1: papers-with-extractable-text.** Today: 41 with any text, **12 with full text**, 32 producing cards. We need ~120. Paywalls are real. **If the first corpus run does not clear 100 papers-with-text, STOP AND FIX ACQUISITION BEFORE WRITING A SINGLE WORD OF PROSE.** If we top out near 60 papers / 250 cards, my honest estimate is **0.53–0.55 — bodhi class — which is exactly the failure the operator named.**

**Do I believe >0.5578 is reachable? Yes — and here is the specific reason, not a hope.** The judge is *comparative*, and every criterion is now a checklist we can read verbatim. cellcog's document is beatable on two criteria it under-plays: **READ-D1** (it has 10 table rows; WhaleCloud proved 35 scores better) and **COMP-5 "Depth and Representativeness"** (cellcog *asserts*; every one of our sentences will be traceable to a verbatim span in a real journal article, and our Scope-and-Methods section can say so). **cellcog's architecture + cellcog's epistemic adjudication + deeper, verifiable evidence + tables cellcog doesn't have.** That is a #1 play. Copying bodhi never was.

But it is contingent on the corpus, and **I will not claim the number before the matrix is green.**
